tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.

Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and querying blueprints in parallel, since they're independent.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and querying available blueprints in parallel.

  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.

    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

1. Create a Mailbox object in the space (typename: org.dxos.type.mailbox)
2. Enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox)
3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and querying blueprints in parallel, since they're independent.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and querying available blueprints in parallel.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

Let me break down the tasks:

1. Create a Mailbox object in the space (typename: org.dxos.type.mailbox)
2. Enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox)
3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and querying blueprints in parallel, since they're independent.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and querying available blueprints in parallel.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

content.text:
Let me break down the tasks:

1. Create a Mailbox object in the space (typename: org.dxos.type.mailbox)
2. Enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox)
3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and querying blueprints in parallel, since they're independent.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and querying available blueprints in parallel.

jsonSchema.description: An employing organization of a person.
An organization.
jsonSchema.title: Organization

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

content.text:
The inbox blueprint is enabled. Now I need to create the mailbox properly. The schema requires a `feed` field which is a reference to an `org.dxos.type.feed` object, and a `filters` array. Let me first create a feed object, then create the mailbox with a reference to it.

But wait, I don't see `org.dxos.type.feed` in the schemas list. Let me try creating one anyway, or maybe I need to check if the feed can be created as an expando type.

Actually, looking at the schema more carefully:
- `feed` is required and is a reference to `org.dxos.type.feed`


- `filters` is required and is an array

I don't see a feed schema defined, so let me try creating an object with typename `org.dxos.type.feed` directly and see if that works.
The inbox blueprint is now enabled. Now I need to create the Mailbox. The schema requires a `feed` reference (to an `org.dxos.type.feed` object). Let me first create a feed object.
The feed was created with DXN `dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTDR`. Now I can create the mailbox with a reference to this feed.
The feed object was created. Now let me create the Mailbox with a reference to that feed.
The Mailbox object has been created successfully. Now let me call read-email on this mailbox.

All three steps completed successfully:
1. Mailbox object created in the database
2. Inbox blueprint enabled
3. read-email called and completed successfully (returned empty content, as expected with no messages)
All three steps completed successfully. Let me summarize and complete the job.

success.summary:
All completion criteria met:

1. **Mailbox object created** — A Mailbox (org.dxos.type.mailbox) named 'Test Mailbox' exists in the database at dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTFD, with a linked feed object at dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTDR.

2. **Inbox blueprint enabled** — org.dxos.blueprint.inbox was successfully enabled with no rejections.

3. **read-email called successfully** — The read-email operation completed successfully on the mailbox, returning empty content (as expected with no messages in the feed).

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  # Email extraction
  Parse confirmation emails (e.g., flight bookings or hotel reservations) into structured objects.
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

Let me start by creating the mailbox and querying blueprints in parallel, since they're independent.

</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  # Email extraction
  Parse confirmation emails (e.g., flight bookings or hotel reservations) into structured objects.
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

Let me break down the tasks:

1. Create a Mailbox object in the space (typename: org.dxos.type.mailbox)
2. Enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox)
3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and querying blueprints in parallel, since they're independent.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and querying available blueprints in parallel.

content.text:
The inbox blueprint is now enabled. Now I need to create the Mailbox object properly. Looking at the schema, the mailbox requires:
- `feed`: a reference to a feed object (org.dxos.type.feed)
- `filters`: an array of objects with name and filter properties

I need to first create a Feed object, then reference it in the Mailbox. Let me check if there's a feed schema available. I don't see org.dxos.type.feed in the schema list. Let me check if I can query for it or create it differently.


I'm wondering if I should try creating a mailbox without explicitly setting up a feed first—maybe the system will auto-create it—but the error indicated feed is required. Let me attempt to create a feed object directly, even if its schema isn't showing up in the list.

The inbox blueprint is now enabled. Now I need to create the Mailbox properly. The schema requires a `feed` reference (to an `org.dxos.type.feed` object) and a `filters` array. Let me first check if there's a feed schema and try to create a feed object.
There are already feed objects in the space. I can use one of them as the feed reference for the mailbox. Let me create the mailbox with a reference to one of those feeds.
There are existing feed objects in the space. I'll use one to create the Mailbox.
The mailbox was created successfully. Now I need to call read-email for that mailbox.
The Mailbox object was created successfully with DXN `dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTF5`. Now let me call read-email on it.

The read-email call completed successfully with empty content (no messages), which is expected. All three criteria are met:

1. A Mailbox object exists in the database (id: 01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTF5)
2. The inbox blueprint was successfully enabled
3. read-email was called and completed successfully (returned empty content)
The read-email call completed successfully with no messages (empty content), as expected for an empty mailbox. All criteria are met. Let me complete the job.

All three completion criteria have been met:

1. **Mailbox object created in the database**: A Mailbox object (typename: org.dxos.type.mailbox) was created with name 'Test Mailbox' and DXN dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTF5. It references an existing feed object and has an empty filters array.

2. **Inbox blueprint enabled**: The blueprint with key 'org.dxos.blueprint.inbox' was successfully enabled. No errors occurred.

3. **read-email called and completed successfully**: The read-email operation was called on the mailbox and returned empty content (no messages), which is expected for a fresh/empty mailbox. The call completed without errors.

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  # Email extraction
  Parse confirmation emails (e.g., flight bookings or hotel reservations) into structured objects.
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  # Email extraction
  Parse confirmation emails (e.g., flight bookings or hotel reservations) into structured objects.
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.

Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
